claims.claim: Commercial ridesharing underserves small-town university settings, producing a multi-faceted coordination gap that extends beyond transportation; CampusRide formalizes existing grassroots practice into an identity-verified multi-module campus platform grounded in a formative N=117 carpool survey.
Commercial rideshare (Uber/Lyft) has documented availability and pricing gaps in small-town university settings.
claims.purpose: Beat 1 (F1 Gap vs. Substitute) honesty check

claims.claim: International students have self-organized grassroots coordination via WeChat/WhatsApp groups across multiple domains including carpooling, marketplace trading, and activity organization.
Institutional .edu identity verification provides meaningful trust uplift in carpooling preferences (65.9% WTP in N=117 formative survey).
claims.purpose: Beat 3/6 (F3 .edu as Trust Primitive) honesty check

claims.claim: Drivers in a campus peer carpool setting show lower tolerance (26.6) for unfair ratings than for late passengers (42.7), changed routes (44.8), or changed destinations (33.0), resonating with algorithmic management literature.
Gamification is a secondary motivator for carpooling (48.3%) behind financial motivations (63.6%) but above social (45.6%) and environmental (44.5%) in the formative survey.
CampusRide is presented as designed and implemented; no deployment data is provided to validate the four design decisions in the carpool module.
